text: Regular endurance exercise over several weeks produces significant adaptations in skeletal muscle oxidative capacity through increased mitochondrial volume and function.
Physical inactivity and hereditary conditions decrease mitochondrial volume and function in skeletal muscle, reducing endurance performance at both high and low intensity levels.

Chronic endurance exercise triggers mitochondrial biogenesis through a signalling cascade involving muscle contraction signals, activation of nuclear and mitochondrial DNA transcription factors, and post-translational protein import into mitochondria.
Regular endurance exercise can attenuate age-related decline in mitochondrial content and delay progression of diseases associated with aging and physical inactivity.
Improved mitochondrial volume and function through regular physical activity provides previously sedentary and aging individuals with improved quality of life and greater functional independence.
